0.right:
There are two matters to consider when acquiring a business through interest:

First, the shopkeeper should be a Muslim.
Second, the prices offered should be reasonable.

For both of these endeavors, seek the counsel of a storyteller in the market.

Bashir’s Store

Now, the store offers everything a customer could desire – fine goods at affordable prices, such as shoes, mufflers, locks, towels, sweaters, soap, perfumed soaps, nails, perfume, laundry supplies, and more…

Dar al-Khilafah
Rifa al-Islam

Monthly Magazine
Pashtun
Peshawar

Abdul Ghaffar Khan
Editor

Number :-

It is desirable to inquire how other nations progress in the acquisition of knowledge and the arts; if you wish to live with honor in the world, do not act as a thief; treat yourself as a stranger if you seek familiarity; if you wish to see yourself with a hundred black teeth like a mullet; and always strive to reach your destination without risk.

Manzoor Elahi Press Peshawar

1.right:
Pashtun 2 June 1928

If any individual seeks to expand their shop and business,
they should advertise within the pages of *Pashtun*.

How to do so effectively:

*Pashtun* is a publication circulating throughout the border provinces, printed in vast quantities and reaching even the most remote towns. It is widely read by Pashtun men and women alike, serving as a vital source of information for the Pashtun people. It is considered a trusted and reliable voice throughout the world.  It is held in high esteem and is never discarded.

Why advertising should be conducted responsibly:

Advertising must be truthful, reflecting the nature of the goods or services being offered. It should not contravene religious or moral principles, nor should it unfairly exploit the caste, religion, or interests of others.  Furthermore, advertisements should be published in Pashto, and if translated from another language, should be rendered with clarity and precision.

How to place an advertisement:

You may submit your advertisement to the *Munjeer* (Editor) for consideration for inclusion in the advertising section of *Pashtun*.

Pashtun 3 June 1928

2.right:
June 1928, p. 4 Pashtun

Ghazal of Badshah Ahmad Shah Abdali

My heart was shattered by the sight of two beautiful women, a storm of longing has seized it.
For that reason, it remains restless, consumed by this turmoil.
This is not a fault of their beauty, but a fault of my own heart.
The number of my longing before you is a thousand times greater than my own.
If I counsel my heart, the pain will be unbearable.
I will face the world, and face it again, facing the world’s vastness.
When I stood upon the mountain of my strength, my heart was locked in its power.
This poem is born from the tears of my soul, a testament to your beauty.
What market could I find, today I stole your beauty away!
Hasan Baj, who did not kill him, held the world captive in his gaze.
I say I will not love, my heart is powerless without you.
It was not love that brought peace to my soul, only your presence.
My heart has no resolution, no rest until it beholds your beauty.
2.left:
The renowned saying in Pashtun culture is that the year of the ox is known from its gait.
Amir Ghazi, in all his words and deeds, if one were to carefully consider every aspect of his life, would surely understand that this man was created by God with a particular disposition and for a specific purpose. He did not acquire this love and affection for his people through mere trade or profit, but rather, God bestowed upon him this quality – a trait not born of Pashtun ignorance or animosity.  Any individuals within a tribe who have sacrificed their lives for two acres of land, and have betrayed their brothers for the sake of a small gain, will inevitably develop a sense of loyalty and affection for those who have stood by them.
Amir Sahib is of this tribe, and he is well aware of this, yet he remains deeply devoted to his people.  Allah has bestowed upon him this love, and it is evident. It remains unknown how this national love was achieved.

3.right:
Pashtun
4
June 1928

Ghazal of Badshah Ahmad Shah Abdali

A heart, it seems, has been fashioned from two roses,
Their petals, like bells, a restless beauty they possess.
This is no fault of the Zulfo’s, nor a lack in their beloved’s grace.
His face is warmed by the sun, his heart, a radiant sun.
If I touch your heart, banish this burning pain from me.
Facing the spring, he faces the world with pride.
Within his strength, his heart is steadfast, a fortress of resolve.
This book has drawn tears from my eyes,
What market was there, in my heart, as I gazed upon your beauty?
Hasan Baj, he demanded tribute, holding the world hostage.
I say, let love not consume my heart, let it find peace in you.
There is no place for my heart, no solace for these beloved’s lashes.
The entire world seems to reflect his glory.
Ahmed’s grief is a familiar sorrow.
5
Pashtun
event
Volume 1
1928
Corresponding to Dhul-Hijjah 1346
Number 1

The National Love of the Shah of Afghanistan

(From مولانا عبدالمجید افغانی)

A common saying among Pashtuns is that in the year of the cow, one cannot discern the seasons.
If one examines the words and deeds of Amir Ghazi, and considers every aspect of his life, they will surely understand that God created him with a particular disposition and for a specific purpose, and in his heart resides the love and loyalty of his people – this is not gained through mere acquisition, but is a quality bestowed upon him by God.  The ignorance and animosity of the Pashtuns are not the cause of this; any affection gained through worldly possessions and petty concerns is viewed with suspicion. The individuals of any tribe who sacrifice their wealth and livelihood for a small profit and dwell solely on trivial matters will not possess genuine love for their people.
Amir Sahib is of this nature, and he recognizes it well, yet he remains consumed by the love of his people, earning the epithet of ( *yuhibu-hum wa yuhibbu-hunah* ) – “God loves them and they love Him.” It is unknown how this national love was achieved.

4.right:
Pashtun
6
June 1928

Not entirely, he stated it himself.
* It is this love that caused the rebels of Khost to stand alone, heads bowed, without eyes. He then said that the leader possessed a pure spirit, akin to the divine, and that he could not abandon him.
* And this was a testament to the steadfastness of his love, evident in the bonds forged by these two or three agreements, with their weapons exchanged and offered for fortune and a throne. Should this be done, we would not repeat it.
* This also reflected the impact of his love, witnessed during his time in Jalalabad when the border tribes were compelled to leave for Europe, resulting in bloodshed, and he was considered a beautiful and unblemished soul, beyond the reach of shame in their religion. He instructed them that no one should blame them for their actions.
* The Amir’s character was thus, and he was aware of it, but he nonetheless held onto this love for his people, adopting the epithet “Uhibbu wa Yuhibbuhu” (He loves and is loved by God) – a fitting title. It is difficult to fathom the source of this national affection.

5.right:
June 1928, p. 8 Pashtun

I – approximately thirty men.
Amir Sahib – I offer my greetings! How long will you remain in our lands?
I am but a novice, barely a year past my youth.
Amir Sahib – You shall acquire many valuable lessons here, and learn all you can. Come to Kabul, and let us work together. It is a lamentable thing that I have not yet seen the fortunes of all the Pashtuns. Please convey my warmest greetings to Talu.
Whenever we spoke with His Highness, it was clear that he held us in the highest regard.
The people cried out, proclaiming that the English King possesses a magic – a charm that ensures prosperity and good fortune.
I, however, am but a simple man, and a brother to you. Should I perish, my spirit will return to the homeland, to the place of my kin. But you, my friend, shall take my place. Yet, let it not be said that the man will not appear, nor will anyone deny that every man in the East, whether rich or poor, knows every aspect of his own homeland, and the life in Europe is a spectacle – one that few wish to repeat. The rulers of Hindustan, such as the King of Iran in Persia, have merchants of perfume in Cairo.
Amir Shahid did not travel to Europe; he merely sent a few inquiries from Bombay. And everything was received with great interest. But Amir Gazi undertook a journey to Europe that was unlike any other – he arrived in his carriage, and for the first time, he looked upon his men with such affection. Upon the carriage’s movement, the loyal Pashtuns cried out, urging him to hasten to Amir Sahib, who immediately ordered the carriage to stop:
“Amir Sahib, do you understand Pashto?”
“Yes, my Lord, we are Pashtuns.”
“Please extend your hand and make a fresh, sincere oath.”
“Amir Sahib, come to Kabul.”
“Yes, my Lord, we are here to work together for our homeland.”
“How many Pashtuns are present in the land?”

6.right:
Pashtun June 1928 10

For the sake of others –
I will address this objection as well, though I understand Amir’s planned trip to Europe will conclude before long. It’s still not entirely clear whether the Pashtuns will endure if they do not continue to live in the homeland. Should they survive, we can then consider how to rebuild and sustain our lives. In “The Future” Pashtun, I foresee a situation where the material-minded people will express their gratitude to Amir, while the spiritually inclined will pray for his well-being and support. 
Within this, I record a remarkable event that occurred with an elder, Sharif, in Ajmer, in the year 1306 – and has remained unchanged to this day.

He fashioned a garland of flowers upon his chest.

In the world, God is the Lord of the gardens – for the leaves turn to red flowers in every direction you look, and the air is filled with the fragrance of blossoms. Now, souls awaken in every person, and they breathe in the fresh air, experiencing joy. The arrival of spring is announced by the yellowing leaves of the orchards. A line of trees forms on the golden lawn. Then, a vow of happiness is made by the bubbling brooks. Let it not matter if storms gather in the garden. The breeze intends towards the garden, and he fashions a garland of flowers upon his chest. 
The worshipper of Nimr worships the sun again, witnessing the thread of the sun’s rays. New love is found in the Parwana flower.  He burns himself, sacrificing himself for his beloved. This is because Hamid witnessed the power of the Creator. 
Mian Abdul Hameed, a student of the Jamaat Islamia Collegiate School –

7.right:
Pashtun 12 June 1920

His homeland is Kashmir – a paradise for every man. He can spend his days freely within its borders. It is a sad tale: a hunter captured a pigeon, took it home, and placed it in a beautiful cage, cherishing it with immense affection, not allowing it to be unhappy. It cried and wailed until, finally, the hunter returned it, overjoyed to have his beloved bird back. The pigeon then drifted to the riverbank, sometimes submerged, sometimes emerging, struggling against the current. It was tossed about, soaked and exhausted, until finally, it succumbed to the waves and sank beneath the surface. Despite this immense suffering, it remained happy. The hunter, seeing its plight, inquired if it was a grand palace or a fine house? It was no different from the original, yet the news spread far and wide. This is Kashmir – the homeland of every Pashtun.
7.left:
Color is the dearest dream of every man, his homeland, whether it be in the Garo hills or in distant lands like Egypt. You may not realize it, but even these powerful nations – Egypt and others – are willing to sacrifice their own heads for the sake of freedom and justice. If there is no justice, there will be no war, no corruption. Life is best in peace, not in strife. We remain unaware of how the events of Yaghestan have unfolded – how villages were brought to prosperity and the eyes of thousands of impoverished people were taken from their homes, a sorrow that continues to this day, as we may be forced to draw our borders with Afghanistan, robbing the people of foreign lands of their freedom, plundering their wealth, and destroying their homes.  Oh, the mountains! What is in their high passes and hidden treasures?

=== NEXT ===

8.right:
Pashtun
             14 June 1928

                   Finance and the Summer Season

God’s will and man’s desires are not the same. Human beings, guided by imagination and speculation, should base their actions on the truth regarding God. As much as human thought progresses, it becomes clear that this year’s harvest will be abundant. The prosperity and growth of the crops represent a fleeting ray of happiness for the impoverished. A dangerous greed fuels the desire for strength and the peace of the heart. For God did not intend this. The provision decreed by Him is distributed to those deserving, and it reaches them as it is meant to. As the crops ripen, happiness inevitably transforms into sorrow. The poor and landholders are not only deprived of water for their sustenance but also lack provisions for the common good. This breeds further sorrow and worry regarding the impending tax burden. It is undeniable that the tax collector’s office will be emptied and relieved of its duties, yet even amidst this hardship, mercy and justice should prevail in the world. Compassion must be shown towards the plight of the poor, the humility of the impoverished, and the vulnerability of the landholders. Careful consideration must be given to such a barren and unproductive year. The landowners should not expect any benefit from the harvest. The Sarkar, with due diligence, should ascertain whether the crops have yielded or not. Indeed, if the harvest is meager, it is imperative that the government refrain from imposing taxes, for without a harvest, the source of the tax revenue remains uncertain. The proceeds from the sale of livestock or other goods belonging to the landowners cannot be relied upon, and if they are sold, how will they sustain themselves? This is a painful reality in Punjab, where the Sarkar has offered no kindness. We earnestly request that the Sarkar extend its compassion to the people of Punjab. We hope for their mercy.

9.right:
Pashtun 16 June 1928

The Mardan Landowners’ Association
Jama Khan! Peace be upon you.

We humbly submit these few lines for publication in *Pashtun*, offering our sincere gratitude. We have observed the considerable distress felt by the residents of Mardan due to the difficulties faced by the landowning community. In order to alleviate these hardships and to chart a course towards progress and betterment, a public meeting was convened on the tenth of May, 1928, at which the Landowners’ Association was formally established, with Khan Sahib Sarafraz Khan of Mardan elected as its presiding officer. The objectives of this Association are as follows:

A. To maintain a stable and orderly system of land management, ensuring the proper conduct and oversight of officials responsible for dealing with landowners.

B. To seek through official legislation avenues for the advancement of landowners and the improvement of their livelihoods, diligently exploring paths towards reform and prosperity.

We, Muhammad Ashraf, Secretary of the Landowners’ Association, respectfully request that the name of the Association be rendered more clearly by utilizing the Pashto term rather than a transliteration. (Driver’s Note)

10.right:
I have already bequeathed to you this cycle of worship and the ethics of religion.
What did you do about it, what will you do, and where are you going?
If any Khan claims a hundred thousand in interest, then the Mullah Sahib will consult the Sharia to determine the answer. Now we do not know what the Khans will say, and where the government speaks of education, this is offered as a service to you. What do you do about the reforms I have previously suggested?
Do what you wish now. You do not concern yourself with the management of your own “bar room.” You cannot simply insist that a Hindu Muslim be his own guide; it is not proper for you to decide that one lawyer will demand ten rupees, while another will provide only five. Your secretary will handle all your affairs for five rupees, and at the end of the matter, you will consider his judgment. As for the dispute over a club, it is not possible to improve the condition of an Islamic school. If you cannot reach the head of those endowments, how can you manage the entire province? This is still a small matter to consider, and the Hindu Muslim is caught between parties. If they offer you more, God knows what will happen next.
Now we are perplexed as to what the educated men will answer these questions. We are merely observing a spectacle, neither in the houses nor in the government. “Foreign war is but a half-day.” If the Khans abandon the field, we will become sailors. If the government speaks of education, we will wear trousers and enter. The name of our nation comes from us. Idle talk is of little value:
The government possesses an iron bullet – look at the teeth of steel.

The tenth annual meeting of the Atmanzo Association “Islah al-Afaghna” was held. A sermon of welcome was delivered.
In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful – Praise be to Him, and peace and blessings be upon His Messenger.
But now, first I would like to express my gratitude to my esteemed guests, who have traveled a long and arduous road to attend this meeting. I offer my sincere thanks and express my appreciation for any kindnesses they have shown the association. The sympathy they have displayed is evident in the increased enthusiasm and dedication of the workers and staff. May Allah grant them the appropriate reward. Although the association has not provided services commensurate with the needs of its members, we still hold hope that it will not unduly burden them. For each person has their place. Another matter is that it is difficult to maintain a clear mind in the times of such factions. The Islah al-Afaghna Association is aware of this difficulty and has accounted for the total income, expenses, outstanding debts, and educational status of the previous year, which are presented for your consideration. Secondly, it is important that the citizens of this country – the residents of a nation – become familiar with their national rights, for the law of nature dictates that every soul has a natural inclination towards love of country and home. This is the inherent nature of every living being: to find peace and security in their own homeland, in their own home. It is a matter of necessity that each inhabitant of a land possesses the power to protect and defend their homeland, and it is a natural right. Looking back from the past to the present, we see that the protection and management of a country do not require small or petty fortifications, nor is there any need for a single test to pass an examination. Rather, a few strong and capable individuals must be gathered together as a center of leadership.

11.right:
Pashtun – June 1930

The history of the Arab era of ignorance reveals how small the world is in comparison, a mere cave or desert, with inhabitants living a rudimentary existence, governed only by the leaders of each tribe. These leaders possessed limited power and no formal civil war within their own communities. However, under the sway of the Persian Shahs and the Roman Caesars, a sense of national unity and shared connection arose, centered around respect for the House of Allah and the willingness to sacrifice personal wealth and honor for its protection. It was a common belief that the honor of the House of Allah rested upon the shoulders of its people. Whenever an external threat arose, all tribes would swiftly unite and agree with the foreign power. Conversely, during times of strength, the people felt themselves unjustly oppressed and unfree.  The absence of institutions for higher learning, such as universities, further hindered their progress.  Most tribes lacked a national identity and did not require the resources of a broader society. This is not due to a lack of freedom, but rather a fundamental right – the self-preservation and protection of one’s homeland, a natural and inherent right of every nation’s people. This is not a claim of superiority, but rather the necessity of certain natural qualities, such as courage and a central authority, for a people to gather and unite.  Once a nation’s core characteristics are corrupted and its natural rights are violated, its progress and honor are inevitably lost. God’s eternal decree states: “Indeed, Allah does not change a people until they change themselves.”  This law underscores that the fate of a nation – its honor and its progress – is determined solely by its own actions.  Until a nation abandons its inherent qualities of bravery and justice, and neglects its own natural rights, it will not experience prosperity or decline.

12.right:
Pashtun June 1928, 22

It is desired to protect the homeland from the influences of foreign powers and the passion for sacrifice, which is always present in the hearts of its inhabitants – this is the primary cause of its true success. During the conquest of Mecca, a decree came from the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, for the Muslims, especially for the refugees – it came in this particular manner: “Those who are exiled from you, remove them from your land” (Sahih al-Bukhari). Many such examples exist of the sacrifices made by the women of the Muslim faith. However, it is regrettable that in our time, such a proportion of sacrifice does not exist among us. If we were able to achieve the spirit of the Companions of the Prophet, we would certainly attain a glorious state, and our purpose would be fulfilled. It is just and fair that the Muslim world, in its entirety, has established equal rights for all people. Islam has decreed that all are equal, but it is known that Islam teaches that the true purpose of life is to promote the faith, and this is the meaning: “Indeed, Allah does not change a people until they change what is in themselves” (Quran 2:25). The true characteristics of a people – their courage, their sense of justice – cannot be altered until they themselves change those qualities. They must not forget their own rights and freedoms. Islam and the teachings of Islam are a gift from Almighty God to the world, and they are intended to establish the natural inclinations of man. All human beings are born with innate qualities, and these rights are inviolable and cannot be forcibly acquired.  Let us not forget this fundamental principle.

Regarding the session of the “Movement for the Reform of Afghanistan,” the address of welcome:

In the name of God, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate – We praise God and send blessings and peace upon His Messenger, Muhammad.  However, after a great deal of effort and hardship, I address you, my esteemed friends, who have come to this session after a long and difficult journey. I sincerely express my gratitude and appreciation for the efforts made in this movement, and I thank those who have shown empathy for the difficulties experienced by its members. While the movement has not been able to provide the highest level of service to its friends, I hope that its efforts will not be overlooked or undervalued.  Since the movement’s activities and expenditures for the past year have been clearly outlined, and since the interests of the residents of Afghanistan – the people of the country – have been brought to their attention, recognizing that the law of nature dictates that every human being seeks refuge and love in their homeland, and that every living being desires peace and security in their own home – it is impossible for any nation to protect and maintain its own territory without the strength and resources of its people.  It is not through small schemes or encroachments that a nation can achieve progress, but rather through the qualities of a few – courage and a central authority – that a nation can truly flourish.

June 1920, 25 Pashtun

As the Prophet said, “The nations will gather around you as the hungry gather around a loaf of bread” (Sahih al-Bukhari).  The Messenger of God, peace and blessings be upon him, when he migrated, he brought his trusted companion Ali to Medina (Sahih al-Bukhari).  He said, “If you wish, I will migrate, but may your head be lowered.” Ali replied, “I am ready to sacrifice my life for you, O Messenger of God.”  And so, the Prophet migrated, leaving his family and loved ones behind.  The Companions of the Prophet, may God be pleased with them, have sacrificed their lives for the propagation of the faith in the same way.  It is regrettable, however, that in our time, this is not the case.  Currently, the propagation of the faith is not even a priority.  However, it is known that God Almighty has decreed that the Muslim Ummah is the best community brought forth for mankind, commanding good and forbidding evil.  Men have a higher status than women, but Islam has established equal rights for all.  Indeed, the most honorable among you before God are those who are most righteous.  The Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, has given an example: “Whoever is among you desires to be closer to Allah, let him spend what he earns” (Quran 92:3).  The Messenger of God said, “The love of the world and the hatred of death are the cause of all evil.”  This is the reason why the south Indian movement for progress fell apart.  Let us now focus on the work that must be done to achieve progress in this world, and let us find the spirit of sacrifice within ourselves, and for this, may the Almighty guide us.

13.right:
Pashtun 24 June 1920

It is a blessing that I am sacrificed for you – specifically, the Prophet’s migration to Medina, where Hazrat Ali was given his rightful place (Bukhari). There are countless examples of Muslim women’s sacrifices, but sadly, we lack that twentieth-century spirit of selflessness. If we could emulate the companions of the Prophet – striving for sincerity, unity, and unwavering adherence to their example – then our situation would surely improve, and we would attain our goals. Justice in Islam grants equal status to all humankind; the saints have been given equal legal rights. Islam teaches that every moral and pious man deserves great respect, as Allah says, “Indeed, the most noble of you before Allah is the most humble of you” (Quran 33:35). The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him) stated, “The nations will gather around you as the devourer gathers to its pit.” A question was asked, “Are we few then?” He replied, “Nay, you are many, but you are a worthless mass, like the debris of a riverbank. And Allah will remove from your enemies their awe of you, and instill in your hearts fear.” The Prophet further said, “It is love of the world and hatred of death.” This is the reason why, in the southern regions of Hindustan, the movement for independence arose during the period of reform – the children’s greed for family luxuries exhausted our patience, and we were never prepared to face any opposition. If we adopt the policies of politicians and embrace Islamic politics, we will be close to the day when we achieve success – and may all our final words be praise to Allah, the Most Merciful and the Most Wise.

14.right:
Pashtun 26 June 1928
Enter beyond the gate. Similarly, the mention of equality in this hadith is of great importance.
The necessity of preaching – the brothers of other religions, today, will raise objections to Islam without reason, and Islam will appear in their eyes in a single, distorted manner. What is the reason for this? If I consider it, will I find that there is no preaching occurring within us, or will it be done in a way that yields nothing but loss? For this reason, non-Muslims do not see the true form of Islam, and this is a matter that has long been concealed.  May God be just.  He would not instruct his beloved in such a manner, nor would he reveal it in the Holy Qur’an.  Indeed, there are many verses within it. However, I will not transcribe the length of this passage. If it is necessary to preach, then God Almighty will not tell his beloved in this way. And in the Prophet’s hadith, he said, “Deliver what I have been commanded to deliver.”  Even if there is only one verse to convey, it is known that the greatest task of the Prophet was to preach, and all the prophets that we see sacrificed countless injuries and hardships for their faiths, sacrificing their lives for the sake of their religion, but they ceased preaching.

Pashtun, June 4, 1920

Hazrat Umar and the River Nile of Egypt
(Brother Abdul Ghafoor)
What a pure soul was he, the chief of both worlds, whose qualities are renowned. It is said that in the age of ignorance, such individuals possess immense power and pride and honor, but in the Islamic era, they possess even greater. This caste, a blessed person, is the caliph of the two worlds, having surpassed all others. And because of his unwavering faith, he was known as Farooq – meaning “the one who separates good from evil.”  His name, Farooq, was bestowed upon him, a testament to his righteous character. 17.right:
Pashtun
32
Pashtun Reform
Abdul Rab Khan Saheb Fakhr. B.A., LL.B. (Alik Vakil, Peshawar)

Initially, the British administration or the region of the Frontier established a system of governance and began to close temporary dams, followed by legal prisons in 170-174 and then another in 196-197. In addition to these restrictions, a significant undertaking was undertaken to gather information regarding all tribal lands, both hereditary and spiritual, and to impose restrictions on their transactions. This was done with the unfortunate result of imposing restrictions on all tribes, repeatedly expressing the grievances of the Pashtun people in their language (Na'u'd by Allah min zalik) in these transactions.  This injustice, inflicted upon them, provoked a terrible reaction, and God’s punishment was sought for their actions.

Islam has accomplished many great deeds throughout the world, and one of its most significant achievements is the establishment of laws to protect women from oppression, which was rampant before its arrival.  Women were treated as mere possessions, stripped of their human status, but Islam enacted numerous laws to elevate them to the same level as men.  This resulted in the widespread expression of these grievances in the Pashtun lands, repeatedly documented in their customs and traditions.  These practices, reflecting a disregard for the Sharia, were frequently lamented and condemned.  The Pashtun man, consumed by honor and shame, would often find himself in a state of despair, his grief manifesting as a devastating flood, leaving him bewildered and ashamed of his own helplessness.  

Let me, as a leader and brother, address you, my fellow Pashtuns, and express my willingness to submit to the guidance of a wise and just leader, should one arise.  Yes, we are the oppressed, the picture of misery, and it is our duty to seek redress through the Sharia.

18.right:
35 Pashtuns
Refinement and Cultivation of Jalili
Introduction
The Pashtuns, who have suffered unexpectedly from the plague and the devastating effects of the country’s disease, are dying of hunger and illness – their faces pale with exhaustion.
Yazan, the most learned of the learned, prepared himself diligently from the very foundations – he cultivated his heart and mind, becoming a keen observer of Hanuwi’s customs and traditions, offering counsel and advice to its administration and aid. He recognized the need for education and the cultivation of knowledge within the Pashtun people.  He sought to prepare them for the challenges ahead.  He understood the importance of both military preparedness and the arts, striving to guide the nation towards prosperity.

Pashtun development was never a spontaneous affair.  It was a deliberate and carefully considered process.  The hope was for a complete and flourishing Pashto.  The knowledge and wisdom of the Pashtun people were essential to this endeavor.
(Chur Muhammad Imamin Selakhleh and Muhandad Lathe Pashtun)

34 Pashtuns
A Voice for Pashto
But if a simple Pashtun poem is presented,
The Pashtun recognizes his Pashtun heritage and the youth of Pashtun are eager to learn Pashto.
Let us cultivate Pashto and the youth, providing them with a solid foundation in the language.
The need for Pashto knowledge and education is paramount.

Pashtun development requires a dedicated and earnest effort.  Let us strive for this goal.
(Chur Muhammad Imamin Selakhleh and Muhandad Lathe Pashtun)

19.right:
Pashtun 37 June 1928

Alone in the world, let us turn towards no one save ourselves! (A lament)
My heart aches – there is no balm for this wound.
My face is a mask of sorrow, a wretched image of man.
The shadow of my father’s house weighs heavily upon me – a drowning fate.
I am consumed by anxiety, devoid of the joy of childhood.
1. O Allah! There is no one like us in this world, burdened as we are.
Grant us your mercy and kindness, O door of salvation for the oppressed and the destitute.
It is not known to us the extent of our failings – yet your grace extends even to those who oppress us.
Your wrath does not stir, your anger remains dormant.
When, then, will our hearts, so burdened with sorrow, finally find release?
Let us not be consumed by despair, let us not be overwhelmed by the weight of our suffering.
(They) yearn for your grace, and fear your wrath.
Let us cry out, “Save us!”
May I, on the day of Judgment, lament my fate.
Then, in the hereafter, my heart will be laid bare.
I seek refuge in God, for how can I conceal my grief?
Why does my heart yearn for solace?
Look down upon the plight of the helpless.
No one but you can be my refuge.
2. Let me weep, let me reveal the secret of my own despair.
The world has turned its back on me, my family is estranged, my brothers are distant.
They have passed me by, blind to my suffering.
O great God, what do I do?  What is my fate?

All: “Yes, yes, certainly, certainly!”
Leaders! This is the story of the oppressed, the images of their suffering that reside within your heart.

20.right:
The eyes of those men were the color of the night, and their hearts found joy in each other. They were the source of Dari’s comfort; the shadow of mercy that the father spoke of, and the paradise the mother described – a sickness now, leaving us orphans. Age, they say, is a name unheard of at such a time, a burden borne with patience. We, innocent and untainted, have wept a river of sorrow, a lament without end.

What has become of my father’s loyal friends?
What of those companions who would always call us to their side and sacrifice for us?
What of the pleasant company, the youthful camaraderie?
They all gather, seeking to see you no more.

What will become of you, always seated at the table?

Help, friendship, kinship, mercy, sympathy – all are absent.  As if all Muslims once considered their own children worthy of such affection.

Cruelty is known among some, a stain upon Islam, a mark of the orphan.
June 1929, page 39. Pashtun

“Saying, ‘Ahlul Dil Se Shitada Islam Ke’ – Islam begins with the heart.”
There is an end to the suffering of the orphan in Islam.

The believers, in those days, the words of one father were the children of one family, the blood of one body. Now, brother turns against brother, and wealth against the poor.

What happened to my father’s dream friends?
What happened to those friends who would always sit beside him?
Who will always call us, and sacrifice for us?
What happened to those pleasant companions, those young men?
They all come together, seeking to see you no more.

Then what will become of you, always seated at the table?

Help, friendship, kinship, mercy, sympathy – nothing remains.

I remember the days of my father, the friends he had, the gatherings we would attend.  Now, I long for the past, for the warmth of those connections.  I weep, and the tears flow endlessly.

Let us not be consumed by grief, let us not be burdened by sorrow.
Let us not be consumed by the bitterness of loss.
Let us not be consumed by the weight of our misfortune.

Let us not be consumed by the bitterness of loss.
Let us not be consumed by the weight of our misfortune.

There is no one to guide us, no one to offer solace.
We are lost in the mountains, sitting at the doors of hunger.
My mouth is parched, my throat dry.

21.right:
June 1920

At last, the joy and happiness we had longed for – it was fulfilled. We remained, alas, forever unskilled in the arts, lacking occupation and perfection; we had no fingers to tell the stories of our fathers, and we could not seek other means. We passed through fortune…
Indeed, time can erase all things, forbidding one to speak of their woes.

A Traveler’s Hour

I penned this poem on May 24th, 1920, in London, on this very date. The people’s hearts were weary – my heart, pure and unblemished, yearned constantly. I was happy that you were there, that it was Eid for you all. At this time, I recalled my ancestral village, and the suffering of my neighbors filled my heart with sorrow. I banished this grief, returning to my own quietude, lost in thought. The following words flowed from my pen:

Let the house remain peaceful within my heart – let my entire being become a cluster of red poppies. My heart does not desire to return to my homeland; it fills me with shame.

22.right:
June 1928

A falsehood! A lie! Do not speak of falsehoods, nor of the secrets of the house.
It is not right to seek revenge on someone by invoking their father’s name.
What is this you are doing? Do not cast blame upon others by claiming they have done you harm.
I cannot bear to be entangled in a thread, and so many men have been made for me. No work, no sustenance, and no grain.
The house is filled with the sounds of the loom and the bleating of sheep. The barber looks like a great honor to him.
If you look at two things, then all the people will be deceived. The elder brother is not released. The girl is foul-smelling.
There was no other answer than to cry. He cried for countless times.
He promised that after an hour, Marjan Khan would arrive and unleash his wrath upon me.

(3)

She sat in her father’s lap, the black eye of all.
Even now, anyone with magic on their forehead will cease to speak.
They will gather together on the occasion of Eid wherever their old friends are. Gul Muhammad, the breeder of birds, was famous in his village.
Alas, the traditions of the Pashto language had made him miserable. At the age of fifteen, he married a boy nine years his junior. His father
With his silver hands, he placed the money on the table and took it away. After a month, her coat was removed, and now she was a little girl.
Jabai Ganga, the mountain of sorrows fell upon his head. There was no place for the father. Her husband called her a spider. Now she was old. The whole stone was useless.
The centuries will pass, the run will be ringed. Tola Verkh will be busy at work. No, the Biga did not even have an eye patch.
.......... After researching the clothes, the shroud would not be placed upon him, for he was not worthy.
Pashtun 75 June 1928

You cannot cross a standing bridge if you do not have two legs.
On top of the bad luck, there were many green scars. One day, Sanjar, shaking his head, placed his foot on the foot of the lotus to stand up.
He would talk about his old friends wherever they gathered on the occasion of Eid. The elder brother was known in his village as a breeder of birds.
But alas, the traditions of the Pashto language had made him miserable. At the age of fifteen, he married a boy nine years his junior. The father
With his silver hands, he placed the money on the table and took it away. After a month, her coat was removed, and now she was a little girl.
Jabai Ganga, the mountain of sorrows fell upon his head. There was no place for the father. Her husband called her a spider. Now she was old. The whole stone was useless.
The centuries will pass, the run will be ringed. Tola Verkh will be busy at work. No, the Biga did not even have an eye patch.
.......... After researching the clothes, the shroud would not be placed upon him, for he was not worthy.
The day was full of flies on the green body of the room. The wind blew into the room, carrying a grain of sand in Marjan’s eyes.
Kayla is thankful that few people died.
Makhotan had arranged a meeting with Gul Muhammad Khan in the last night. With the help of Kod Dasdut Lag, the heart of Ra Lode Verbandi was handed over:-
“Why is your back against me?” he asked. “It is not yet open to talk about it, and you are like that.
Are you dead? Enter, hand washing.”
Marjan slapped him and said:
“Do not say anything to him, Dad. Leave the mill alone, whether you join the party or not.
If it doesn’t work, I will do it myself.”
In such an hour, the birds will be thrown and the hands will be washed by hand. Ran Marjan gave him a stone in the chest that looked poor.
Khange Radghorzadeh. Marjan was:
“That’s enough, I will wear my husband’s khilaat myself.
If not, do you marry Nawab?”
The bird turned its eyes full of tears and said:
“My son, where am I?”
Marjan raised his toe:

23.right:
Pashtun 47 June 1920

“Are you red with anger, people? Look at what I have done to you!” he exclaimed. Outside, he bowed his head, yet the room was empty of salt – a stark sign of his misfortune. It is said that the people of the region have conspired against him, haven’t they? Since that time, the bonds of kinship have grown strained, and the whispers have multiplied. Gul Mohammad Khan, having heard these reports, addressed his wife: “You used to boast of a bountiful harvest, but now you are merely a nose! Do you truly believe that you can still claim to be a part of this community?” The old man, burdened by grief, felt the weight of his sins pressing down upon him. The pale light of his eyes reflected the darkness of his sorrow, and he felt no relief, only a deepening despair.

24.right:
Pashtun 28 June 1925

The mother observed a strong grain of sputum, one that had been laid upon the walls between the houses. The bird, it seemed, had ceased its lament. The mother, refusing to touch the wedding garments due to the sins of her brother, inquired, “Why do you speak of sins, brothers?” The mother, whose heart had been deeply touched by the bird’s cries, held it close, safeguarding it with great care. She asked, “Why do you wear the wedding clothes? Have you not removed them from the sin?”  God’s grace then touched the father’s heart, and the woman, reflecting on the womb’s gift, declared, “There is no fault, no error.” The bird’s heart, overwhelmed with grief, could not even weep, for the tears had dried.  As the garments fell upon the room, they scattered, and the tears spilled forth, striking the face. The girl, overwhelmed, returned to her path. The species passed, yet the bird remained unaware, nor did it recall anyone. “Who remembers sins?”

25.right:
Pashtun 50 June 1929

Announcement
Mr. Shah Abdul Rahman Sahib, a prominent figure, has generously decreed a reward of fifty rupees per page for the publication of this magazine – the lifespan of each page should not exceed two sheets, nor surpass five; the content of the article should be concise and to the point, and a decision will be rendered by three impartial judges. Please submit your essays to the office by August 15th, and the results will be published in the September issue of Pashtun.
Mian Ahmad Shah, Barrister-at-Law, and Dock Khan of Peshawar

A Few Words with the Authors
(1) Articles should be written in clear, accessible Pashto.
(2) Utilize vocabulary appropriate for everyday use – avoid overly ornate or scholarly language.
(3) Particular attention should be paid to matters of religion, morality, and refinement.
(4) Articles that denigrate or promote harmful habits are not welcome.
(5)  Stories and proverbs that offer instruction and guidance should be presented in elegant and concise prose.
(6)  Learners are encouraged to study the script from the pages of “Pashtun,” paying particular attention to the feminine script.
(7) Essays should be printed on separate sheets of paper.
(8) The editors retain the authority to adjust the length of each article as needed.

June 1929 51 Pashtun

Genuine and Imitation, Affordable and Expensive
It is crucial to exercise prudence when conducting business.
That is, do not purchase counterfeit goods, nor should you seek out inexpensive items that appear costly. Remember the saying: “An inexpensive item without a reason, and an expensive item without wisdom.” The genuine article is typically less costly than its imitation – a genuine item may cost one rupee, while a counterfeit one may cost eight.  This disparity is particularly evident in the case of long-term investments.  Therefore, be discerning and avoid being deceived by inflated prices.

Furthermore, the magazine is pleased to announce the continuation of its efforts to promote the benefits of Islam for the Muslim community and to encourage trade and progress.  We strive to provide a wide selection of goods, including textiles, spices, grain, pulses, and all other commodities of the realm, offering fair prices to the Muslim merchants of India.  Their management and organization are of paramount importance.  Let us not be swayed by deceptive practices or inflated claims.

Bashir’s Star Storybook
At the present time, fine quality goods of all kinds – socks, handkerchiefs, sweaters, vests, all colors of ribbons, soaps, oils, perfumes, powders, and various other items – are available at reasonable prices from the esteemed Mr. Nabi.  He is the sole provider of these goods in the market.  In addition to the beauty of the Pashto language, the freshness of its fruits and vegetables, the aroma of its spices, the sweetness of its dates and figs, and the abundance of its various products, it is truly a haven for the discerning shopper.  We invite you to visit and experience the quality and affordability firsthand.

26.right:
Sadiq’s New Emporium, Atmanzai
Haji Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Haji Shanwaz Khan, Proprietors

Pashto is a new Muslim for the benefit and encouragement of trade and development; therefore, we have released the Makar, tobacco, grain, level, and all other goods of the country, all types of the nation’s merchandise, readily available for sale, and rupees are paid to Indian Muslim traders for arrangement. Sir, what price are you seeking for your goods?

Furthermore, a second emporium has been established for the benefit of the Sarmano; only merchandise of the finest quality is sold there. Credibility has diminished from this venture. Do not purchase fake highs and lows, and be mindful in the market.

Bashir’s Black Storytelling
Today and yesterday, here in Peer Naba, goods like Vera Ali Ale sweaters, hats, mufflers, loafers, and socks are readily available and inexpensive in beautiful designs for sale. In addition to the fragrances of Pashto, tablons, perfumes, mapolon, and various musical instruments are plentiful.

The slave of the late Peer Naba
The free time of the free
(Servant)

Monthly and Yearly
Pashtun
The month of Rabi’ al-Awwal, 1929

List of Verses
Greetings from the poets regarding their verses.
We are attempting to improve and refine them, and to praise their work. It is often exaggerated. We release them for the benefit of all readers, and therefore, we offer this greeting.
Let Pashto verses be released. It is necessary that in them there be
And many more verses that are quick and beautiful. Clear and pleasing. Let us publish them quickly. Let all Pashtuns
Not be preoccupied with idle thoughts. Many are the merits of this. And if the printed matter
Is a joy. Then that is the joy of the compiler. Therefore, we offer this greeting, that the dignity
Of the Pashto language be preserved. Let the merchants and others purchase from us. Do not speak ill of us.

28.right:
January 1929

Allah,
A leader of such renown,
And what fate awaits our homeland?
Of the Baduyya, a life of high esteem,
A spirited spirit, swift and keen,
As long as the Muslim’s influence prevails,
Two noble minds have blossomed, a sight to see.
In this case, I, though yearning for Peshawar,
Do not dwell on it, my heart in the fields,
If freedom is your ambition, your association’s goal,
A religion, a god, a faith’s decree.

(Servant)

The Poets’ Plea,
A request offered for the betterment of Pashtun poetry. We strive to refine and improve it,
Often exaggerating in our praise. We are printing this,
For the benefit of all our brothers, as a humble offering.
May Pashtun poetry be sent forth,
And may its articles be numerous and swift, clear and pleasing.
Let us publish it immediately, all Pashtuns,
That no one should harbor a false notion of its worth. And if the meaning of this print,
Is joy, then let it be so. Let us further argue, in this submission, for the dignity of the Pashtun people,
Returning to the Mullah and beyond, and purchasing from the Pashtun buyer. Do not speak ill of it.

29.right:
January 1929

Pashtun

Also appeared. A salutation to the District Commissioner upon presentation is not out of self-control or courtesy. Consequently, we resolved that greetings are a mark of respect. There is no difference between our independence and a simple Salaam. Greetings are, indeed, required. For it is our goal to find individuals of good character and high morals. Upon arriving at the District Commissioner’s bungalow, I first heard Okara. I did not proceed to the police station. Instead, I found myself seated on a black and white chair, a solitary figure. A greeting was offered, and I noticed a dark shadow by the river valley. Mr. Orboz, observing me at times with a troubled expression and at others scrutinizing his own dark face, called out to me. He had journeyed to Kabul, and the government granted him permission to return to the country, though he did not accomplish anything. Yet, he was relieved of his sorrow. The player expressed his displeasure, addressing me with a sharp rebuke. He inquired as to how I had returned home. I explained that this was also a matter of concern for our nation, O Dalam. Regardless of where my heart resides, I would return to this land. I implore you, do not burden me with further concerns. Later, Mr. Sahib’s anger became palpable. He turned his face dark with displeasure. He demanded that I follow a particular example.  He repeated the same request several times. (more on that)

(feathers)
January 1929

Pashtun

Therefore, let us not pretend that we have not spoken to anyone these days, nor have we seen eye to eye. Our thoughts, however, were misguided and forbidden.  We must now come forward and make amends for our past mistakes, allowing ourselves to be guided by reason and courtesy. We are a household concerned with expediency. What we need to do is to act with prudence and consideration. Suddenly, the machine arrived – a sign of the approaching year. In this year, Professor Maulana Abdul Hamid Dal arrived.  Upon greeting him, I observed a look of profound regret in his eyes. At that moment, a poem came to mind, and I felt compelled to recite it, despite my unfamiliarity with poetic expression. The poem read: “We are those impoverished countrymen who, dying and disappearing, are taken as shrouds by others.” Maulana, I have spoken. Let us now proceed with this decision.  Abdul Akbar Khan provided bail, but as of now, he is not a member of the Association, nor of the Congress or the Dakhla Fat. He is only subject to accusations leveled by the Bolsheviks. And we were, therefore, associated with the seals of the Khilafat and the Congress. The decisions of these congregations are the current desires of the people of Qul. It is imperative to seek the assistance of the bailiffs regarding bail. For we are, above all, servants of the people and upholders of this order. What, then, should we do?  Let us, therefore, seek their counsel. This is the question.

30.right:
Here’s an improved translation of the current spread, aiming for fluency, coherence, faithfulness, and an appropriate early 20th-century register:

**امن**
(3)
This is the peace for which the tribal people do not suffer oppression. However, Sahib Delhi grows larger year by year. This is the peace that provides sustenance to the impoverished populace. And this is the peace that maintains a vast army of police officers throughout the country. Indeed, the government should be solely in the hands of the government itself; peace should not be established in the southern regions. It becomes a matter of curiosity as to why the men of the Mardan region, who once enjoyed a period of tranquility, now find themselves subjected to such lengthy delays.  The government’s order is clear: that no one should be detained, and that all should return to their homes.

**Peace**
(13)
This is the peace for which the people of Afghanistan do not suffer oppression. However, Sahib Delhi is growing larger year after year.  A group says that our salvation lies in this Commission, while another says that it will only leave and abandon our country.  A Commission was sent to the most important place, and it is being welcomed.  And we have presented an objection in the service of our people.  Regarding the welcome and objection, it is said that practical pen will decide.  Despite the noise and clamor of the river, it is always like a snake. But we must not forget the grievances and complaints that have always been our rights.  Therefore, let Gazi Amanullah be praised, not the Commission, nor the reports of the British.  Rather, let the Grand Assembly (Parliament) present the decisions of the people to the ruler.  I will express my opinion about the ruler of Afghanistan, that he is a brave man and a servant of the people, in another place.  Not the opinion of the British, nor the advice of the doctors.  Let the tax collectors and the beneficiaries go to the aid of the poor and look at their work.  Let the disputes and objections be resolved, and let the noise and clamor cease.  May God guide, but he does not know what to say.

Islamia College and the Literary Circle

(The student, Fazal Haq, Shahi Mahmoud Abadi, has established a Literary Circle at Islamia College, which will begin accepting applications on November 29th.  It is a great blessing that our young men are beginning to love their own language and culture.  May God bless the opening of this college and may He succeed in their purpose.  Amin. –  Further news will follow.)

**The Method of Education**

Education is divided into different stages. Each part has a specific name.  Those in the lower grades are called (Alphabet). Those in the upper grades are called (Growth). And those in the highest grades are called (Excellence). This is the order of the classes (A, B).  The divisions of the communities are as described above.

This version aims for a more polished and readable style, while retaining the original meaning and tone.

31.right:
January 1929

Education in Afghanistan

A Period of Development

Various subjects pertaining to Afghanistan’s diverse fields are regularly published in the *Sarmal* newspaper each week, and I believe these offer valuable insights into the current era. I would like to express my appreciation for the guidance of Amanullah Khan, whose efforts are proving beneficial, and to share with you news of progress – though I hesitate to boast excessively. My sincere wishes for success in your endeavors. However, the most reliable measure of intention and effort is the dedication of resources to education. Therefore, Afghanistan’s Shah is justly regarded as the most commendable among all tribes.

Pashtun
10

The Simon Commission

There is unrest throughout the country, with the people gathered in groups. Some claim that our salvation lies within the Commission, while others argue that it will merely leave our country without offering a solution. A Commission was dispatched to Mobah Saran, receiving a warm welcome, and a boycott was presented as a demonstration of loyalty to our people. The matter of reception and boycott will be addressed through reasoned discussion and consensus. Despite the constant noise of the river, we must not allow past grievances to cloud our judgment. Let us remember Ghazi Amanullah and the Commission’s reports, rather than dwelling on past disputes. The Loya Jirga (Parliament) should be entrusted with the welfare of the people. I will elaborate on this matter in a later section. It is not the duty of the British to provide medicine to the Afghan people; rather, it is their responsibility to uphold justice and fairness. Let the grievances of the common people be addressed through proper channels and resolutions, and let the issues of reception and boycott be resolved amicably. Let us put an end to the endless speculation and uncertainty.

Islamia College and the Literary Circle

(I, Fazl Haq Sheed Mahmood Abadi, a student at Islamia College, am pleased to announce the establishment of the “Bazm-e-Adab” (Literary Circle) on November 29th. May God be praised that our young people are embracing their own language with enthusiasm and dedication. I extend my congratulations to the Principal of the College and wish them continued success in their noble endeavors. – More Love)

Method of Education

Education is offered through various schools and seminaries. The traditional curriculum is divided into three stages: *Ibtil Shieh* (the foundational stage), *Rashid Yeh* (the growth stage), and *Aali Dih* (the advanced stage).
31.left:
Pashtun
12
Various schools exist for different sciences, and they are named accordingly. Here is a description of each:

*   **Telegram Office:** Work related to telegrams is conducted here. (Electric Telegraph)
*   **Map Office:** The work of drawing maps of countries is done here.
*   **Agriculture Office:** The science of agriculture is taught here…
*   **Trade Office:** Work related to trade is conducted here…
*   **Metal Office:** Various languages are taught here, such as Turkish, French, English, and others…
*   **Government Office:** The art of governance, administration, and the interpretation of laws is taught here, though it is not always successful. Then the work of the government is carried out. This means that governors are appointed. The principles of the British government are a good example. The branch of the school is…
*   **Principal’s Office:** This is a school for administrators. It teaches the art of administration. It is in accordance with the *Ting Kalam* (the spirit of the nation). The Darul Uloom Arabiya (Arabic Seminary) teaches religious studies, philosophy, and other subjects.
*   **Military Office:** Military training is imparted here, and all work related to the army is conducted here.

This is a list of all the schools for each science. In addition to these, there are also a few smaller institutions for general education. They are…

---
Pashtun
13

**The College of Habibullah Khan (deceased)**

This college, established in memory of Amir Habibullah Khan, offers instruction in geography, history, literature, mathematics, and theology. Turkish and English are also taught here. These are the established levels.

**The Abadi College:** This college is also established in the same manner. The method of education is also the same. Only here, German language instruction is given in particular.

There are three hundred schools in Afghanistan and another fifty are being established. All students in Afghanistan are currently enrolled in schools. Unfortunately, other nations do not provide the same level of education. Only the children of the wealthy are afforded this opportunity. Therefore, it is hoped that the Shah of Afghanistan will provide education freely to all.  The Afghan people are considered the most advanced in the world in terms of education, meaning that all people will become literate and able to read and write. In schools for ten years, the Afghan people will be in opposition to the half-educated Indian people, a people known for their civilization and knowledge of science and government, living under the rule of a government with a high level of progress. Provided that the enemies’ malicious schemes and oppression are overcome.

(The above is a sincere expression of my hope.)

32.right:
Pashtun
January 1929
12
Various schools exist for the study of different sciences, and each is designated by its specific name. Their details are as follows: the Telegraph Office, where telegrams are dispatched. (Electric Telegraph) Here, the maps of the countries are drawn. The Agriculture Office, where the knowledge of agriculture is taught… The Trade Office, where work related to trade is conducted… The Muqat al-Banna, where various languages are taught, such as… German, French, English, and others. The Governors’ Office, where the administration of the government, legal matters, and regulations are taught, though its effectiveness is often lacking. Consequently, the government operates. That is, rulers are appointed. The principles of the English government serve as a model. The school branches are like limbs. The Usul Office is comprised of workers and staff, working in offices. Darul-ul-Mulameen is a seminary for representatives; it provides substitute education, following the example of the Darul Uloom Arabic, where logic, philosophy, and other subjects are taught. Military education is also imparted here, and all matters pertaining to the army are addressed. This constitutes a list of the schools for each science. Furthermore, there are few places for general education; they are limited…

33.right:
Pashtun
January 1929

It is thus understood that the people of the Pashtun nation have, for generations, borne the burden of service, sacrificing their own well-being for the preservation of their community. The deeds of many remarkable women in history attest to this dedication – women whose efforts were paramount to the survival of the Pashtun people. While I will not delve into exhaustive detail, I will mention a few of these exemplary figures, whose courage and devotion are worthy of remembrance. It is a simple truth that this perception of our people has shifted. We once believed that only through self-sacrifice could the nation endure.

I state this not as a new realization, but as a correction to a long-held misconception. It is not merely for personal desires that we seek fulfillment, but for the service of our people. The demands of the present moment far outweigh any individual aspirations, and we must therefore fulfill our duties accordingly. It is not something that will be apparent to you. Indeed, only through selfless service can we truly benefit the nation. This service extends beyond the battlefield and into the realm of ‘jihad’ and ‘aman’ – both in times of war and peace. Should we succumb to weakness, our children would likewise be weak. If we embrace a spirit of betrayal, our offspring will inherit that same treacherous path. Therefore, no son should ever sacrifice himself for the sake of his people or his country. Within this piece, I wish to express my desire for the progress of Afghanistan in terms of civilization, education, and justice – all of which are currently prevalent in the West (Europe).

34.right:
January 1929

A Call to Service and Community

It has long been a notion I held, a troubling one,
That women of our Pashtun people, with unwavering devotion,
Sacrificed themselves for the preservation of their nation,
Working tirelessly, shoulder to shoulder, for the survival of our people.
Though many tales of healing exist in history,
I shall be brief, mentioning only a few exemplary figures,
Known for their wisdom and compassion in this era. Let it be known.

I speak only to dispel a false impression from my own mind.
That these desires, born solely of personal longing,
Required no training or dedication. And were not aligned with the needs
Of their creator, as they are prevalent in the present time.
No further responsibility lay upon them. If it did not appear to you,
That these desires were not for self-fulfillment, but rather for the service
Of the nation in times of both war and peace. If a woman were cowardly,
Her children would be cowardly as well. If she wished to sacrifice herself
For the sake of the people, she would not be aware of the secret.
So she never prepared her children to sacrifice in the way of the nation
and their country. In this article, you will see that the Turks have faced
a difficult time. Constantinople was under the control of the Allies
(British, French, Italian). Our nation’s servant stood for us.
A test it was, like his name is also on the list,
Included in the list of those captured by the enemy.
There was a reward of thousands of rupees for capture. He always
handed over his children to the army, with his head bowed,
taking the British path. And the enemy joined our army,
seeking to enlist in their ranks. And you will see such a blessed woman,
who, though little was known of her in her time, was attuned to the conditions.
So let it be known. ‘Children! Arman is still waiting,
I wish you a blessed day. What you
And their heads fall in love on the way to space. And so on
Why, I will give you my wool. If you
Disloyal to our country and people. You will understand the same.
What a swallow! Above all, they serve the country and religion.
But I do not mean this from the article. I desire civilization, education,
and rights as much as those prevalent in the West
(Europe).

35.right:
January 1929, 18
Pashtun

Considering the present state of affairs and the Western (European) advance, which posits that “the middle is the measure of all things,” I desire a balance that does not disadvantage either our nation’s condition or that of the West. I implore you to initiate actions beneficial to our religion and country. And the land of our ancestors was a reward bestowed upon those whose natural inclination lies with another people. For her, this play is a beautiful pursuit to engage in with others. Let us not be consumed by the weather and its floods. Let us prevent the natural elements of the weather from being turned away from our own land and placed upon foreign feet.

Today, we consider Pashto our heritage. Let us educate our sisters. However, I pose this question to my people: what if Ferangi influence continues to grow, and the necessities for it increase? For if education is curtailed and the free atmosphere of Europe is denied, such expectations cannot be fulfilled. This ill-educated youth will inevitably seek refuge in the embrace of foreign lands. The truth is this: what if these grievances are brought into our homes? Then we will dispel them, and ultimately, what is the consequence? It is imperative for us to…

19 January 1929
Pashtun

Or, if we cease educating boys, or if we construct a bridge that aligns with their needs.

Another matter deserves consideration: our current state. Let us cast aside the notion of splitting lentils. If a war is unavoidable in the future, let it commence. Then, what will we do? We must rely on ourselves. If we falter and are defeated, it is not a cause for dismay. What if these shameful sentiments are discovered within our own homes? Then we will confront them and, in the end, what is the outcome? It is necessary for us to…

…begin in a manner that does not step on the rights of others, nor does it allow the principles of grief to dominate. Let us not allow the weather to be turned away from our land, nor should we allow it to be subjected to foreign influences. 

--- PASHTO SOURCE (for reference) ===

36.right:
January 1929
Dream
I am dreaming of the “month of freedom” – Edpa
I awoke from impotence – I say now in Pashto, interpreting it as best I can.

39.right:
January 1929

26

The Result of Bingo’s Freedom

In whichever room they resided, the floor was of polished wood – it was the ceiling of another chamber, where a single, ornate scroll lay.  It was said to have been brought from Kandahar, a vibrant green branch. The second, from Kabul; the third, from Nethori; the fourth… and so on, ascending upwards.  As a result, the color shifted and changed.  A strange, unsettling effect.  The owner of this room, a man of considerable wealth, was known for his extravagant tastes.  He would spend vast sums on carpets, silks, and jewels, all displayed with a showy pride.  It was said that he’d purchased a great many fine garments, and that his household was filled with servants.  He would gaze upon them with a critical eye, and his displeasure was swift and severe.  He would often summon them to his presence, demanding attention and obedience.  His servants, fearing his wrath, would tremble at the mere mention of his name.  My Pashtun people, I am prepared for any hardship, for any misfortune that may befall us.  Let us strive for freedom in our homeland and in the realm of Islam.
39.left:

Finally, the final step has been revealed…  He has prepared himself to be close to his leader.  He has undertaken this sacrifice for his sister.  . . .  They are stubborn and unyielding; their voices rise in complaint, as if they were constantly arguing.  They waste their time on empty pronouncements, and they thank God for every small blessing.  Some scholars are exceedingly kind to us, we are held in high esteem.  How little respect do they show us?  How do we, as men, become so corrupted?  It is as if a dark stain has settled upon our souls.  I saw something strange in my eyes – a fleeting image.  The tears from my eyes flowed, like the tears of my ancestors.  The people of my tribe will not be shamed; they will not be brought low.  In this time, we will not send letters or messengers.  We eagerly await the welcome of those far away.  The tailor and the clothesmaker, both devoted to my comfort, have crafted garments of exquisite beauty.  The words in my thoughts, expressed in verse, are now written on paper.  The wise man has advised against excessive pride and vanity.  Let us not be led astray by the allure of wealth and luxury.  Let us not be consumed by envy and malice.  Like the two rivers, they flow in opposite directions.  Those who speak of such things are often regarded with suspicion.  I have offered my prayers to the heavens, seeking guidance and protection.  I have seen the faces of those around me, and I have avoided their gaze.  The noble character of my Pashtun people is not diminished.  We are ready to face any hardship, any misfortune that may befall us.  Let us strive for freedom in our homeland and in the realm of Islam.

42.right:
January 1929

32

Pashtun

A grievous loss is lamented, and each Englishman strives, with pen, state, and all means, to foster the growth and stability of his own dominion.

It is imagined that six crore guarantors dedicate themselves to the service of any kingdom. And similarly, men are sought—each possessing the wisdom of Aristotle in thought, and the strength of a hero in action. How can such a kingdom fail to advance day by day, and to command respect and authority? Its power has brought the entire world beneath its heel, and its banners have risen alongside those of Europe.

Yet this very power, which has raised nations to prominence, has also subjugated countless peoples, laying claim to forty crore territories under the pen—a legacy known as Hindustan. What further evidence demonstrates the dominion of these rulers across the globe? And justice demands that they be placed in their rightful positions, serving as the cornerstone of every nation. They have bowed the heads of all beneath their weight, and their hands have united them in a single chain.

(What has been done? The common folk dreamt of prosperity. And the Ferangi, seeking to learn from their own successes, established schools for their own benefit. And of these...

January 1929

33

…lessons, a distinguished curriculum was established. The people gathered in groups to improve their skills. And (a, b) they would judge who excelled. Then they would bestow upon them degrees of learning and accomplishment. Such a middle group was formed, a stepping stone for those who wished to progress. Those who passed would then be taught the principles of ruling their own lands and their own wealth. This was the final cohort. Yet, no one passed the examination, and no one among the students was deemed worthy or qualified. It is said that those who remember their rights and the teachers declared that the students were called upon to take the examination for the entire day, but none succeeded.  Let them strive with all their might to be deemed worthy of the examination.  Thus, they prepared themselves for the final assessment – Qa to Qa....)

The question then arises: in the past, powerful empires dominated the world. But today, they look to their own example for guidance, and emulate the British in their efforts to serve their country and kingdom.  The difference between them and other nations is thus evident.  Like a merchant who has amassed experience and wealth through years of trade, so too do these rulers.  And other nations, striving to follow in their footsteps, remain far behind.  This is the reality—that in the present era, where there is peace and security within the home (internal), and influence from abroad (barai), and the pressure of the Ferangi, others benefit from these conditions.

34
Pashtun
The Heart of a Muslim Prince of Granada
Reports
Murder Story 5
Then the Tombs of Both Were Laid Together
At the dawn of the day, the two would sit side-by-side. Subsequently, five distinct chambers were sealed. Saeed, accompanied by Jamil Beh Khalku, was captivated by the imaginative visions of the lilies. What a wondrous sight! Such a Christian, and he practiced that faith – Christianity…
Florinli Saeed, who was diligently excavating the tomb of May, was profoundly affected by the undertaking. The impact of each moment was far greater than mere conversation. He remained undisturbed throughout the night, resting upon his bed. Consequently, a profound stillness descended. As the sun rose, they proceeded to the tomb of Bani Ajr, joining Saeed in contemplation. He sought to understand his own restless spirit. But he often lost himself in thought, imagining what Saeed felt for him. Saeed and Florinli bin then proceeded to the cell of Qasr al-Hamra. However, there was no light upon Saeed’s face. This occurred at times, but not always. Rather, he was consumed by sorrow. This was because Florinli had spoken to him yesterday concerning the cell of Qasr al-Hamra. I recall my promise to you, taking notes. We are shattering that heart into fragments. He was lost in thought, his heart pounding with anxiety.
Pashtun
January 1920
35
It was not. And this is because he lacked experience in governance. He has witnessed the passage of countless times, and his home remains untouched. And the peoples of other nations do not surpass him in this path; it is a clear example of his and other tribes. Like a man who has spent his life engaged in commerce, accumulating experiences and achieving perfection. And another man, unwilling to enter that trade, will find it so when it is offered. Undoubtedly, we acknowledge that today all have progressed. This is an example of the advancement of himself and others. We also acknowledge that today, volumes are free, and who is free now? He asserted that the world has learned a lesson from…
43.left:
The following is a tale of the world’s most powerful rulers, and their ambitions. But the tale is not of conquest, nor of dominion. It is of a single man, Saeed, and his entanglement with a woman named Florinli. Saeed, a man of considerable wealth and influence, found himself increasingly drawn to Florinli, a woman of striking beauty and intellect. Their meetings were frequent, and their conversations profound. Yet, Saeed was haunted by a persistent restlessness, a feeling that he was perpetually on the verge of some great discovery. He would often wander through the city, observing the lives of others, searching for some clue to unlock the mysteries of his own existence.

44.right:
January 1929

Each daily meeting, whatever its purpose, was fulfilled. Now, Flournelli’s companionship will be realized – a joyous assembly concluding this evening. Saeed, in his heart, was filled with astonishment every hour – “O God, if I am severed from these further misfortunes, will I not also rise?” He was lost in thought, Flournelli saying, “The Citadel of Al-Hamra is where I am.” Saeed raised his eyes, and exclaimed, “Allahu Akbar! How magnificent is this great fortress! It is evident that the sky stretches above its high walls, and the towers, reaching towards the heavens, are positioned in rows along the windows. The head and foot, laid upon the earth’s floor, appear strangely white.” He mused, “One might imagine a diverse array of flowers blooming in the gardens of paradise.” Saeed observed the scale of the construction, and the grandeur of its architect – a balm for the foot’s misfortune. And this verse: -(Arabic poetry)
“And I stood at Al-Hamra, a pilgrim and a tomb, a collection of diverse souls.”

Then he spoke, “O Hamra, is there a return?” And the fortress replied, “And does he return who has died?”
“Indeed, the era consumes all things swiftly; alas, it does not return.”
Therefore, let us examine their memories – let us read their inscriptions! It is a pity that my weeping and lamentations have yielded no benefit; for the spirits of those who have passed are like radiant stars, recalling a diverse assembly.

Saeed now found himself before a desolate hearth. He observed the floor beneath its corner, a series of rooms stretching along its sides, seemingly conversing with the sky. Saeed pondered, imagining that he might have resided there in the past, or perhaps, his gaze involuntarily overflowed with sorrow, drawn by the sight of the floor’s exposed beams.  He felt a longing, a desire to dwell within that space, to experience a moment of respite, or to gaze upon the sky from its lofty heights.  But Florenli’s school did not find this melancholy fitting.  Soon after, it was heard that Florenli was deeply engrossed in the detailed drawings of the building’s architecture.  He therefore proceeded to the hidden, inner chambers, considering the fate of the arched doorway – a matter of considerable deliberation.  Each time he reached the bed, he would glance at the inscription affixed to the door, and it would strike him with a sudden, overwhelming feeling.  Then the voice of Yabta (O father-in-law) would escape his lips, and he would fall unconscious, overwhelmed by a multitude of sensations.

Then he spoke, “O Hamra, is there a return?” And the fortress replied, “And does he return who has died?”

Therefore, let us examine their memories – let us read their inscriptions! It is a pity that my weeping and lamentations have yielded no benefit; for the spirits of those who have passed are like radiant stars, recalling a diverse assembly.
